summary: Stancil contrasts ambition-driven careerism with work chosen for its intrinsic value, arguing that communities thrive when people show up for the work rather than the payoff. He uses dbt Labs’ Coalesce as a rare, genuine data community that feels like college, while acknowledging that scale and business pressures will eventually erode that culture. The piece ends with a call to appreciate the community now, even knowing it won’t last forever.
money_quotes: By separating the work from the reward, we can be more honest with ourselves about why we do the things we do.
When we become fixated on a destination, the journey is an interchangeable means to an end, a disposable rocket booster that’s only value is to put us into space.
The combination of the two has turned Coalesce into one of the last honest gatherings in the data community.
This isn’t a community, but a broadcast, with talking heads and commercials, and an audience wondering if they’re allowed to participate.
No community lasts forever, and the business success that makes Coalesce possible today is the same force that will one day compel it to become something different tomorrow.
tone: reflective and analytical, with a note of wistful criticism
key_insight: Even knowing a community’s golden era is temporary, its value comes from people showing up for the work and relationships themselves, not just the eventual rewards.
